prompt: I want to pass a Rust-allocated String to a C function that will free it. Using mem::forget prevents Rust from double-freeing the memory.
explanation: `CString::into_raw()` is the idiomatic way to transfer ownership to C: it consumes the `CString` (running no destructor) and returns the raw pointer. `mem::forget(s)` is the manual equivalent: it consumes `s` without running its `Drop` impl, preventing a double-free when C frees the pointer. Never use `as_ptr()` without `forget` or `into_raw` when transferring ownership ŌĆö the `CString` would still be dropped at the end of scope.

prompt: I wrap a value in ManuallyDrop to delay its destruction until I explicitly call drop. Show the basic pattern.
explanation: `ManuallyDrop<T>` wraps a value and inhibits its automatic destructor. The value is still allocated (on the stack or heap) but its `Drop` impl will not run when the `ManuallyDrop` goes out of scope. To run the destructor you call `ManuallyDrop::drop(&mut md)` explicitly in an `unsafe` block ŌĆö unsafe because you must ensure it is called at most once and that the value is not used afterward.

prompt: I implement a custom Vec-like type and need to drop only the initialized elements, not the entire buffer. Show using ManuallyDrop for the buffer.
explanation: Custom collection types often hold their buffer in `ManuallyDrop<Vec<T>>` to take precise control of when elements are dropped vs when the allocation is freed. The `Drop` impl drops elements explicitly (e.g., by calling destructors on each slot in `ptr..ptr+len`), then frees the allocation. Without `ManuallyDrop`, the inner `Vec`'s own `Drop` would run automatically, possibly double-dropping elements.

prompt: I try to use mem::forget on a MutexGuard to intentionally leak the lock (poison the mutex). Show the pattern and the safer alternative.
explanation: `mem::forget` consumes a value without running its `Drop` impl. For a `MutexGuard`, this means the lock is never released ŌĆö a permanent leak. This is occasionally intentional in tests or when building lock-free transitions, but must be avoided in production. The safe alternative is always to let guards drop naturally via scope, or use explicit `drop(guard)`.

prompt: I use ManuallyDrop to read a value out of a field without triggering the field's Drop, then drop the rest of the struct normally.
error_message:
error[E0509]: cannot move out of type `Pair`, which implements the `Drop` trait
 --> src/main.rs:17:5
  |
17|     p.name
  |     ^^^^^^ cannot move out of here
explanation: You cannot move a field out of a type that implements `Drop` (E0509) because Rust cannot guarantee the type's `Drop` impl won't use that field. `ManuallyDrop::new(p)` inhibits the destructor. `ManuallyDrop::take` gives back the inner value without running Drop. You are then responsible for dropping any remaining fields you don't need (here `value: i32` which is `Copy` so no action needed).

prompt: I receive a raw pointer from C and want to wrap it in a Rust struct that will free it on drop, without any double-free.
explanation: Wrapping a C-allocated pointer in a struct with a custom `Drop` that calls `c_free` is the RAII pattern for FFI resources. We store the raw pointer in `ManuallyDrop<*mut u8>` to make it explicit that we control the destruction. Rust must not use its allocator to free a C-allocated pointer ŌĆö only the matching `c_free` must be used. The struct's `Drop` calls exactly that.

prompt: I try to use mem::forget to skip cleanup during a panic unwind. Show why this is dangerous and the correct approach with catch_unwind or proper RAII.
explanation: `mem::forget` is not run during a panic unwind ŌĆö the panic bypasses the rest of the function and only runs `Drop` impls. Relying on `forget` for cleanup-skipping is therefore ineffective and dangerous. The correct approach is RAII: implement `Drop` on a wrapper struct. The destructor runs whether the function returns normally or via panic, guaranteeing cleanup in all paths.

prompt: I use ManuallyDrop in a union to hold types with destructors. Show why this is required.
error_message:
error[E0740]: field must implement `Copy` or be wrapped in `ManuallyDrop<...>` to be used in a union
 --> src/main.rs:3:5
  |
3 |     s: String,
  |     ^^^^^^^^^
explanation: Rust requires union fields with non-`Copy` types to be wrapped in `ManuallyDrop`. This is because the compiler cannot know which variant of the union is active, so it cannot automatically call the correct destructor. `ManuallyDrop` signals "I take responsibility for calling Drop". You must track which variant is active and call `ManuallyDrop::drop` on the correct field at the right time.

prompt: I build a slab allocator where slots are ManuallyDrop<T> so I control when each slot's destructor runs.
explanation: In a slab allocator, slots may hold uninitialised or previously-freed values. Using `ManuallyDrop<T>` prevents the `Vec` from automatically dropping every element when the slab is destroyed. The custom `Drop` impl iterates only over occupied slots and explicitly drops them. This pattern is fundamental to implementing arena allocators, object pools, and other custom memory managers.

prompt: I want to convert a Vec<T> into a Vec<U> reusing the same allocation when T and U have the same size and alignment. ManuallyDrop prevents double-free.
explanation: `ManuallyDrop::new(v)` inhibits `v`'s destructor. We then reconstruct a `Vec<U>` from the same raw pointer, length, and capacity via `Vec::from_raw_parts`. The new `Vec<U>` owns the allocation and will free it when it drops. Without `ManuallyDrop`, the original `Vec<T>` would also try to free the same allocation, causing a double-free. The size/alignment assertions are required for soundness.

prompt: I demonstrate that mem::forget is safe (not marked unsafe) and explain why ŌĆö it is the responsibility of the type implementor to handle forgetting.
explanation: `mem::forget` is safe because Rust's memory model does not require destructors to run. Types that rely on their `Drop` impl running (like `MutexGuard`) can be broken by `forget`, but no undefined behaviour results ŌĆö just resource leaks. This is why `unsafe` is not needed. `ManuallyDrop::drop`, however, is `unsafe` because calling it twice is a double-free.

prompt: I implement a into_parts method on a custom struct that has a Drop impl, using ManuallyDrop to extract fields without triggering the destructor.
error_message: error[E0509]: cannot move out of type `Buffer`, which implements the `Drop` trait
explanation: E0509 prevents moving fields out of a type that implements `Drop`. Wrap `self` in `ManuallyDrop::new(self)` to suppress the destructor, then use `std::ptr::read` to bitwise-copy each field out. After `ptr::read`, the original bits in `md` are logically unowned ŌĆö the destructor won't run because `ManuallyDrop` inhibits it. This is the canonical `into_parts` pattern for Drop types.

prompt: I build a scope guard that runs cleanup on drop, and use mem::forget to defuse it when cleanup is not needed.
explanation: A scope guard pattern runs cleanup in `Drop`. Two ways to defuse: (1) set the inner `Option<F>` to `None` so `Drop` is a no-op, or (2) call `mem::forget(guard)` to prevent `Drop` from running. Option 1 is cleaner because the intent is explicit. `mem::forget` also works but is less self-documenting. The `defuse` method is the idiomatic interface.

prompt: I use ManuallyDrop to construct an uninitialized array of non-Copy types for a fixed-size stack.
explanation: `[ManuallyDrop<T>; N]` stores N slots without automatically dropping any of them. The custom `Drop` impl drops only the `len` initialized slots. Using `MaybeUninit::uninit().assume_init()` creates the array without initializing the `ManuallyDrop` wrappers ŌĆö safe because `ManuallyDrop` has no validity requirements beyond its contained type being validly initialized before access.

prompt: I demonstrate that forgetting a value that owns heap memory leaks the memory but does not cause undefined behaviour.
explanation: `mem::forget` on a heap-allocated value leaks the allocation ŌĆö the memory is not freed until the process exits. This is defined behaviour in Rust (safe code can always leak). But leaking in a loop causes unbounded memory growth. The correct mental model: `forget` is a tool for specific patterns (FFI ownership transfer, defusing guards) not a general drop-suppressor. Always prefer letting values drop naturally.

prompt: I use ManuallyDrop to split a struct with a Drop impl into its raw components for an unsafe transmutation.
error_message:
error[E0512]: cannot transmute between types of different sizes, or dependently-sized types
...
explanation: You cannot `transmute` a type that implements `Drop` directly ŌĆö the compiler rejects it. Wrapping in `ManuallyDrop` suppresses the `Drop` impl, making the transmute valid. After the transmute, `ManuallyDrop<Wrapper>` no longer exists; the `Vec<u8>` owns the allocation. This pattern requires `Wrapper` and `Vec<u8>` to have the same memory layout (here guaranteed by the single-field newtype).

prompt: I implement a GC-like system where objects have ManuallyDrop wrappers and a separate collector calls drop at the right time.
explanation: A manual GC stores objects in `ManuallyDrop<T>` wrappers so Rust's automatic destructors don't interfere. The collector calls `ManuallyDrop::drop` only on dead objects. The `Gc`'s own `Drop` impl calls it on remaining live objects when the GC itself is destroyed. This pattern is the foundation of arena allocators and language runtime memory managers.

prompt: I use mem::forget to suppress the destructor of a type that registers/deregisters itself in a global registry, allowing the entry to persist.
explanation: `mem::forget` (wrapped in a `leak` method) intentionally prevents the `Drop` impl from deregistering the entry. This is a legitimate pattern for static/permanent registrations (plugins, signal handlers, service discovery). The `leak` method name makes the intent explicit and Clippy-friendly. The registry retains the entry for the process lifetime.

prompt: I use ManuallyDrop::new and ManuallyDrop::into_inner to conditionally run or skip a destructor.
explanation: `ManuallyDrop::into_inner(md)` extracts the inner value, giving it back its normal ownership ŌĆö including its `Drop` impl. Letting the result fall out of scope (via `let _ = ŌĆ”`) runs the destructor. If `should_drop` is false, `md` exits scope without `into_inner` being called, so the `Drop` is skipped. This is cleaner than the `forget`-in-else branch approach.

prompt: I need to return a raw pointer to Rust-allocated data to C, then later reconstruct and free it. Show the full round-trip.
explanation: `Box::into_raw` is the idiomatic `forget`-equivalent for `Box<T>`: it consumes the Box without dropping, yielding a raw pointer. `Box::from_raw` is the inverse: it reconstructs the Box (and thus the Drop machinery) from the raw pointer. This is the standard RustŌåÆC ownership transfer pattern. Using `mem::forget` manually is equivalent but less idiomatic than `into_raw`.

prompt: I show that ManuallyDrop<T> has the same size and alignment as T, making it suitable for unsafe layout tricks.
explanation: `ManuallyDrop<T>` is guaranteed by the language to be a zero-overhead wrapper: it has the same size, alignment, and ABI as `T`. This makes it safe to transmute between `T` and `ManuallyDrop<T>` and to use them interchangeably in `repr(C)` structs and unions. This zero-cost property is what makes `ManuallyDrop` suitable for performance-critical unsafe code.

prompt: I use mem::forget in an intrusive linked-list implementation to prevent double-free when nodes are removed.
explanation: The code is already correct: `Box::into_raw` when pushing (`mem::forget` equivalent ŌĆö transfers allocation ownership to the raw pointer) and `Box::from_raw` when popping (reconstructs the `Box` which then drops the node). The list's `Drop` calls `pop` until empty, ensuring every node is freed exactly once. This pattern uses `into_raw`/`from_raw` instead of `forget` for clarity.

prompt: I try to call ManuallyDrop::drop twice on the same value, causing undefined behaviour. Show why it is unsafe and how to prevent it.
explanation: `ManuallyDrop::drop` is `unsafe` precisely because calling it more than once is undefined behaviour (use-after-free). The `OwnedMd` wrapper adds a `dropped: bool` flag to make drops idempotent ŌĆö the standard technique for preventing double-frees in unsafe code. This is the same pattern used in FFI wrappers and custom smart pointers.

prompt: I use mem::ManuallyDrop as a field in a repr(C) struct for FFI interoperability with a C struct that has a destructor.
explanation: In `repr(C)` structs for FFI, fields must have predictable C-compatible layout. Using `ManuallyDrop<*mut c_char>` for a pointer field makes the destructor entirely manual ŌĆö the `CRecord::Drop` calls the C destructor function instead. This ensures Rust doesn't call a Rust destructor on a C-owned pointer, and the C destructor is called exactly once via the custom `Drop` impl.

prompt: I use mem::forget to implement a simple reference-count increment without a smart pointer, then show why ManuallyDrop is cleaner.
explanation: `Rc::into_raw` / `Rc::from_raw` are the idiomatic alternatives to `clone + forget + as_ptr`. They clearly communicate intent: ownership of one reference-count increment is transferred to/from a raw pointer. `mem::forget(cloned)` works but is harder to audit. The `into_raw`/`from_raw` pattern is used by FFI callbacks that need to keep Rust objects alive across C boundaries.
